phrasalVerbs.definition: To give support to someone by telling other people that you agree with them.
examples: She backed up her colleague's claims during the meeting.
The evidence backs up his theory completely.
Will you back me up if I propose this idea?

phrasalVerbs.definition: If someone or something blends in, they are similar to the other people or objects around them.
examples: The new building was designed to blend in with the historic surroundings.
She tried to blend in with the crowd at the party.
His accent helped him blend in with the locals.

phrasalVerbs.definition: If something centres around someone or something, they are its main subject of attention.
examples: The discussion centred around budget concerns.
Her research centres around climate change.
The novel centres around a family in 19th-century England.

phrasalVerbs.definition: To become lost or go to the wrong place; to behave wrongly.
examples: Several packages went astray during delivery.
The investigation went astray when false leads emerged.
Good intentions can sometimes go astray.

phrasalVerbs.definition: To choose something as a subject of study or as your career; to enjoy a particular thing.
examples: She's thinking of going in for medicine.
He doesn't go in for team sports much.
I've never gone in for extreme diets.

phrasalVerbs.definition: To choose one person from a group for special attention.
examples: The teacher singled her out for praise.
He felt he was being singled out for criticism.
The award committee singled out three nominees for special recognition.

fillInExercises.sentence: The data ___ his hypothesis about climate patterns.
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Back up' means to support or confirm something.

fillInExercises.sentence: The chameleon can ___ in with its surroundings perfectly.
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Blend in' means to match or fit in with the environment.

fillInExercises.sentence: The debate ___ the question of taxation.
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Centre around' means to have as the main focus.

fillInExercises.sentence: The important documents ___ astray in the post.
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Go astray' means to become lost or misdirected.
'Go in for' means to enjoy or choose to do something.

fillInExercises.sentence: The manager ___ out one employee for special praise.
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Single out' means to select one person from a group.

fillInExercises.sentence: Will the other team members ___ your proposal?
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Back up' means to support someone's position.

fillInExercises.sentence: The investigation started to ___ astray after the false testimony.
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Go astray' means to go in the wrong direction.

lines.speaker: Emma
lines.text: My thesis [centres around] the impact of social media on society.

lines.speaker: James
lines.text: Do you have evidence to [back up] your arguments?

lines.speaker: Emma
lines.text: Yes, but some of my data [went astray] during the transfer.

lines.speaker: James
lines.text: My supervisor [singled out] my methodology for criticism.

lines.speaker: Emma
lines.text: Don't worry—not everyone [goes in for] that approach.

lines.speaker: James
lines.text: Your research style doesn't [blend in] with traditional methods anyway.

lines.speaker: Priya
lines.text: How can I [blend in] better with the team here?

lines.speaker: Dan
lines.text: Just [go in for] the team activities—people will warm up to you.

lines.speaker: Priya
lines.text: I hope I don't get [singled out] as the new person.

lines.speaker: Dan
lines.text: Our culture [centres around] collaboration, not competition.

lines.speaker: Priya
lines.text: That's reassuring. Will you [back me up] in meetings?

lines.speaker: Dan
lines.text: Absolutely. Don't let your confidence [go astray]!
